coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional nanotube-like Fe3O4/PANI/CDs/Ag hybrids: An efficient SERS substrate and nanocatalyst
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Manqing
Shen, Yang
Zhang, Guiyang
Bi, Hong
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, the stable and environment-friendly Fe3O4 nanotubes with polyaniline (Fe3O4 NTs/PANI hybrids) have been prepared via mesoporous anodic alumina oxide (AAO) template, sol–gel method and in-situ polymerization. Then multifunctional Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids have been obtained by decorating Ag nanoparticles by glucose reduction on surface of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI hybrids. The morphologies and structures of these hybrids were subsequently investigated by SEM, XRD, TEM and XPS measurements. The Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids presented high catalytic activity due to the template-assisted presence, preventing Ag particulate agglomeration. Importantly, the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids achieve sensitive surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) signals. Furthermore, the introduction of carbon dots (CDs) endows these hybrids good dispersion and stable photoluminescence (PL). Therefore, the obtained hybrids may have potential applications in waste water treatment, biomedicine, photocatalyst, and environmental analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe3O4 nanotubes (Fe3O4 NTs)
Polyaniline (PANI)
Ag nanoparticles carbon dots (CDs)
Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Fe3O4 NTs within AAO membranes
Synthesis of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI within AAO membranes
Synthesis of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs ternary hybrids
Synthesis of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs/Ag hybrids
Morphology and structure of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI binary hybrids
Morphology and structure of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids
Catalytic property of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids
CLSM images of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs hybrids
SERS of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs/Ag hybrids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of synthesis route of the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs/Ag hybrids.

SEM images of (a) the as-prepared Fe3O4 NTs within an AAO membrane, (b) Fe3O4 NTs stretching out the AAO membrane, (c) Fe3O4 NTs/PANI within the nanochannels of an AAO membrane, and (d) Fe3O4 NTs/PANI stretching out the AAO membrane.
TEM images of (a) the Fe3O4 NTs, and (b) Fe3O4 NTs/PANI liberated from the AAO membranes.
XRD patterns of (a) pristine PANI, (b) Fe3O4 NTs in AAO membrane and (c) Fe3O4 NTs/PANI in AAO membrane.
XPS spectra of the Fe3O4 NTs and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI within AAO membrane for (a) total survey scans; (b) C 1s peaks; (c) N 1s peaks.
Hysteresis loops of Fe3O4 NTs and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI within AAO membranes.

SEM images of (a) Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids, and (b) partially enlarged part of (a); (c) TEM image of the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids, the inset is a magnified image of an individual Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag; (d) the corresponding SAED pattern of Ag nanoparticles in the inset of (c).

UV–Visible spectra of R6G aqueous solution reduced by NaBH4 combined with Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids of (a) 0mg, (b) 1mg, (c) 2mg as functions of reaction time and the concentrations of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids. R6G: 2×10−5 mol/L, 5mL; NaBH4: 1×10−2 mol/L, 1mL.
CLSM images of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs hybrids under bright-field, and at various excitations of 405nm, 448nm, and 550nm, respectively.
Raman spectra of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI, Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs, Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs/Ag hybrids at excitation wavelengths (λ ex) of 532nm.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Manqing Yan and Yang Shen contributed equally to this work.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional nanotube-like Fe3O4/PANI/CDs/Ag hybrids: An efficient SERS substrate and nanocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical engineering, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, the stable and environment-friendly Fe3O4 nanotubes with polyaniline (Fe3O4 NTs/PANI hybrids) have been prepared via mesoporous anodic alumina oxide (AAO) template, sol–gel method and in-situ polymerization. Then multifunctional Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids have been obtained by decorating Ag nanoparticles by glucose reduction on surface of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI hybrids. The morphologies and structures of these hybrids were subsequently investigated by SEM, XRD, TEM and XPS measurements. The Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids presented high catalytic activity due to the template-assisted presence, preventing Ag particulate agglomeration. Importantly, the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids achieve sensitive surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) signals. Furthermore, the introduction of carbon dots (CDs) endows these hybrids good dispersion and stable photoluminescence (PL). Therefore, the obtained hybrids may have potential applications in waste water treatment, biomedicine, photocatalyst, and environmental analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Magnetic one-dimensional (1D) nanostructures have sparked more and more interest due to their importance in extensive applications of drug delivery [1], waste water treatment [2], etc. An ever increasing interest is generated in the preparation, characterization and surface modification of 1D Fe3O4 nanostructures [3] such as nanotubes [4], nanorods [5], and nanowires [6] because of their excellent properties and novel applications. Polyaniline (PANI), a widely-used conductive polymer with low cost, high electrical conductivity, good environmental stability and mechanical flexibility, can provide “hot-spots” for biding molecules due to the presence of amino and imine groups. To achieve efficient performances for applications, a simple and popular strategy was used to synthesize composites containing PANI and various inorganic nanomaterials [7, 8]. In comparison with conventional bulk and other morphologies of PANI, hollow PANI coating shows good suspension ability, high surface areas, and low-dimensional morphologies. Recently, hybrids of PANI and noble metal nanoparticles have generated considerable interest related to their vast potentials in catalysts, sensors and memory devices [7–9].

Recently, surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) has become an increasingly active research in materials and analytical science since sensitive SERS substrate can be applied as an effective tool for chemical and biomolecular detection in comparison with conventional Raman spectroscopy [10]. SERS offers a tremendous enhancement of the intensity of the vibration signals over 10 orders of magnitude, which has opened a new avenue for conventional Raman spectroscopy. It is broadly accepted that electromagnetic field (EM) enhancement and chemical effect are responsible for the SERS activity. Most SERS substrates were designed with noble metal structures (Cu, Ag and Au) since its discovery [10, 11]. Among all of the SERS-active substrates, various Ag nanostructures show a superior SERS performance in recent years [12–14]. More experiments have proven that the extremely high SERS enhancement factors and single-molecule sensitivity come from the compact, non-fractal Ag particle cluster, dimer or larger because the electromagnetic field in their gap region can be drastically amplified [15]. Especially, Fe3O4–Ag composites were usually the center of focus for their convenient separable properties when used as SERS substrates [16–18].

Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as excellent candidates for bioimaging applications due to their strong and stable photoluminescence (PL), low cytotoxicity, good water solubility, excellent biocompatibility and environmental friendliness [19–22]. Previously, ultra-long nanoribbons based on self-assembly of CDs and anionic oligomers have been fabricated and exhibited multi-colored fluorescence as well as electrical conduction [23].

In this paper, we prepared a series of heterogeneous nanostructures of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI, Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs/Ag hybrids by sol–gel method, in-situ polymerization and glucose reduction in AAO template. Importantly, the as-prepared Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary nanomaterials can act as a novel SERS substrate with high sensitive detection and as an excellent green catalyst giving high yield due to the template-assisted presence of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI [24, 25]. Furthermore, Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs/Ag hybrids show multi-colored, strong fluorescence besides a SERS sensitivity. Fig. 1 shows a schematic representation of synthesis route of the various hybrids in detail.
H3PO4, FeSO4 ·7H2O, Fe(NO3)3 ·9H2O, aniline, (NH4)2S2O8, NaBH4, and Rhodamine 6G (R6G) from Aldrich were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Fe3O4 NTs within AAO membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The AAO membranes were firstly prepared by hard anodization process according to a previous report [26]. Secondly, 2.78g of FeSO4 ·7H2O and 8.08g of Fe(NO3)3 ·9H2O with a molar ratio of 1:2 were dissolved in 40mL deionized water, and then 5.6g of citric acid was dissolved in the mixture solution as a surfactant. The pH value of the solution was adjusted to 7.0 by using ammonia, and then 18g of urea was added into the solution [3, 27]. Thirdly, the AAO membranes were immersed in the sol precursor and then heated to 90°C in an oil bath under the protection of nitrogen atmosphere. After immersing in sol precursor for an appropriate time, all of AAO membranes were taken out and rinsed with deionized water and then dried in air. Subsequently, the AAO membranes were thermal treated in a tube furnace at 535°C for 10h under the protection of argon gas. Finally, the resulting membranes were collected after the furnace was cooled to room temperature [3–5].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI within AAO membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared Fe3O4 NTs within AAO membranes were soaked in the aniline monomer under negative pressure for 30min, and then the membranes were taken out immediately and put into a mixture of (NH4)2S2O8 solution (0.18g of (NH4)2S2O8 in 10mL deionized water) and HCl solution (3 drops of HCl in 10mL deionized water). The polymerization proceeded for 2h at 0°C, and then the reaction system was kept for 24h at room temperature [7]. After the AAO membrane was etched for 10min using 0.5M NaOH solution, the product was separated with a magnet and washed with deionized water for several times, and then dried under vacuum for 6h. At last, Fe3O4 NTs/PANI hybrids were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs ternary hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: The typical strategy to prepare CDs was based on our previously reported work [28]. For fabrication of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs ternary hybrids, 10mg of CDs was dispersed in 50mL deionized water under ultrasonic for 1h, and then 50mg of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI hybrids were added into the mixture solution. The mixture solution was heated to 90°C in an oil bath under nitrogen atmosphere for 4h and then the product was separated with a magnet and washed with deionized water for several times. Finally, the product was dried under vacuum overnight and then Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs ternary hybrids were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs/Ag hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: 50mg of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs hybrids and glucose solution were mixed, and then 2mL of fresh [Ag(NH3)2]+ solution was quickly added into the solution. Subsequently, the mixture solution was warmed at 60°C for 5min and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs/Ag hybrids were obtained by separating with a magnet and drying overnight. Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids were synthesized by a same procedure except using 50mg of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI hybrids as starting material.

The morphologies of the obtained hybrids were observed by using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-6700F) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was carried out on an X'Pert PRO diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. Raman and ultraviolet visible (UV–Vis) spectra of samples were recorded using LabRAM HR800 Raman spectroscope and UV759 spectrophotometer, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies were carried out with a spectrophotometer (ESCALAB250). The fluorescence imaging of the products was recorded at various wavelengths by using a confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Olympus Fluoview FV1000 Confocal Microscope).

In a typical catalytic experiment, 1mg (or 2mg) of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids was mixed with 5mL of R6G solution (2×10−5 mol/L), and then 1mL of NaBH4 solution (1×10−2 mol/L) was rapidly injected into this mixture above. The color of the mixture was fading with the experiment proceeding, indicating a gradual reduction of R6G. As a control, R6G solution reduced by NaBH4 solely was performed. The catalytic processes were monitored by using an UV–Vis spectrophotometer [9].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI binary hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of as-prepared Fe3O4 NTs and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI within AAO membrane was investigated by SEM observations [1, 3, 7]. Fig. 2a shows the cross-section of a Fe3O4 NTs-embedded AAO membrane. It can be seen that a typical Fe3O4 NTs array was obtained within an AAO membrane, in which the Fe3O4 NTs are uniform with outer diameter of 175nm and wall thickness of 60nm. Fig. 2b shows a representative SEM image of the Fe3O4 NTs stretching out the AAO membrane. Obviously, some of the Fe3O4 NTs have broken outside of the AAO membrane and crowded together in bundles. As shown in Fig. 2c and d, there is an ultra-thin layer of PANI coated on the surface of the Fe3O4 NTs, respectively. It is noted that PANI coating on those Fe3O4 NTs stretching out the membranes (Fig. 2d) is thicker than that on the Fe3O4 NTs within the AAO nanochannels (Fig. 2c), possibly because of the limited space inside these nanochannels.

The tube-like morphology of the as-prepared Fe3O4 NTs was confirmed by TEM image as shown in Fig. 3a. Moreover, Fig. 3b shows clearly the PANI coating on the Fe3O4 NTs surface in the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI hybrids.

In our experiment, the as-prepared sol was negatively charged due to excessive OH− arising from urea undergoing hydrolysis above 60°C during the sol–gel process. The OH− reacted with Fe2+ and Fe3+ and then formed negatively charged Fe3+(OH−)x(H2O)y and Fe2+ (OH−)x(H2O)y complexes in the sol. It is well known that the pore walls of AAO membranes are positively charged [2, 3, 26, 29], and thus the negative-charged sol would fill in pores of AAO membrane under the osmotic pressure and the capillary force when the AAO membrane immersed into sol solution containing precursor [26, 30]. The sol gradually changed into gel. Once this sol–gel process within nanochannels of the AAO membranes was triggered, sol precursor would continuously transfer into pores of AAO membranes in order to keep the balance of osmotic pressure.

During the gelling process, organic additives was decomposed upon thermal treatment, and then ultra-fine Fe3O4 nanocrystallites formed from the amorphous gels and packed closely. Subsequently, the as-prepared Fe3O4 NTs within AAO membranes were immersed in the aniline monomer under negative pressure and the in-situ polymerization happened. After the conjugated PANI coated on the Fe3O4 NTs surface, 2mL [Ag(NH3)2]+ solution was quickly added into the aqueous suspension of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI hybrids, and then the hybrids with Ag nanoparticles were obtained.

The crystalline structures of the hybrids were characterized by XRD measurement. As shown in Fig. 4 , a broad diffraction peak from 10° to 30° of pristine PANI indicates that it is amorphous. The diffraction peaks at 2θ =18.1°, 30.3°, 35.7°, 43.3°, 53.5°, 57.2°, and 62.8° are in good agreement with the standard profile of Fe3O4, corresponding to the crystal planes of (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) (JCPDS card No. 87-0245), respectively. Clearly, there is no obvious change after PANI coating on the Fe3O4 NTs surface, which may be attributed to the thin, amorphous PANI coated by in-situ polymerization approach [7, 31]. Nevertheless, the diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 NTs and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI within AAO membrane become slightly broadened due to the presence of amorphous Al2O3 templates.

The surface compositions of Fe3O4 NTs and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI were analyzed by XPS measurement. As shown in Fig. 5a, Fe3O4 NTs within AAO membrane are mainly composed of Fe, O, C and Al. The peaks at 118.1eV and 526.3eV are attributed to Al 2s and O 1s of AAO membrane, respectively. And the peak at 285.6eV corresponds to C1s, which comes from the organic additives during the sol–gel process. The levels of Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 are located at 711.6 and 724.2eV, respectively. Compared to Fe3O4 NTs within AAO membrane, Fe3O4 NTs/PANI within AAO membrane shows an appearance in N 1s signal (399.8eV), which confirms the existence of PANI. As shown in Fig. 5b, the binding energy of C 1s of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI (284.8eV) is higher than that of Fe3O4 NTs (284.4eV), which may be due to the strong interactions between the oxygen of Al2O3 or Fe3O4 and imine nitrogen of PANI [28, 32]. The high-resolution scans for N 1s (Fig. 5c) in the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI hybrids could be fitted into three peaks at 398.3eV, 399.4eV, and 400.4eV, corresponding to imine (N), amine (NH), and protonated amine (NH2 +), respectively, which is consistent with the results as demonstrated in literature [28, 33].

The hysteresis loops of the products measured at 300K are illustrated in Fig. 6 . The results indicate that both Fe3O4 NTs and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI hybrids within AAO membrane have high coercivities (Hc ). As shown in the inset of Fig. 6, the Hc of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI (633Oe) is obviously higher than that of Fe3O4 NTs (401Oe). The enhanced Hc of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI may be attributed to the intensive interaction between the Fe3O4 NTs and the PANI [7, 34, 35]. In the meantime, the saturated magnetizations (Ms ) of Fe3O4 NTs and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI hybrids are determined to be 36.4 and 28.1emu/g, respectively. The presence of PANI causes the decreasing mass percentage of Fe3O4 NTs in the hybrids, and thus the lower Ms of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI. It is expected that the as-obtained hybrids with good magnetic performance would have a promising application by a simple magnetic separation in waste water treatment, drug delivery and magnetic resonance imaging [3, 4, 7].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of as-prepared Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids without AAO membranes was investigated by SEM and TEM observations. Fig. 7a is a SEM image showing a large scale of the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids. Notably, Ag nanoparticles with average size of 110nm adhered to the surface of the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI binary hybrids, are still present even after ultrasonic dispersion. That is because of intensive interaction between the Ag nanoparticles and PANI coating. Fig. 7b is a partially enlarged image of Fig. 7a. It can be seen clearly that PANI with net-like morphology surrounding on the surface of the Fe3O4 NTs. Thus, the structural stability of the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI is further improved by a thin PANI coating on the outer surface of Fe3O4 NTs. Fig. 7c shows a TEM image of typical Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids. The inset of Fig. 7c gives a magnified image of an individual Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrid with Ag nanoparticles attached on the surface of PANI. The corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of Ag nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 7d and the diffraction rings are indexed on the basis of the fcc structure of Ag, which are in accordance to the reflections from (111), (200), (220), and (311) lattice planes of fcc-Ag [18, 35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic property of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assess the ability of the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids to be applied as effective nanocatalyst for waste water treatment, the catalytic degradation experiments were performed as shown in Fig. 8 . In the present case, R6G as a model dye was used and its evolution of UV–Visible spectra is illustrated in Fig. 8 at the wavelength of 525nm (λmax =525nm) during the degradation. And NaBH4 acted as a cocatalyst with the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag in the catalytic experiments. As previously reported [9, 36], R6G aqueous solution showed an absorption band at λmax =525nm in the absence of catalysts. In the present study, UV–Visible absorbance spectra of R6G aqueous solution reduced by NaBH4 solely are shown in Fig. 8a as control. Those samples reduced by NaBH4 combined with 1mg or 2mg of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids are shown in Figs. 8b and c, respectively. It can be seen clearly that the degradation efficiency of R6G solution only attained 30% after 3h without Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids. However, the absorbance of R6G solution quickly decreased after adding 1mg of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids (Fig. 8b), while the degradation efficiency of R6G solution increased up to 89% in 10min after adding 2mg of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids into the solution (Fig. 8c). Fig. S1 in Supporting Information shows degradation rates of R6G aqueous solution vs irradiation time in presence of NaBH4, NaBH4 +1mg Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids, and NaBH4 +2mg Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids, respectively. The results demonstrate that the presence of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids can greatly improve the degradation rate of R6G comparable to that of Ag nanoparticles on polystyrene spheres [36].

In order to assess the ability of the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids to be applied as effective nanocatalyst for waste water treatment, the catalytic degradation experiments were performed as shown in Fig. 8. In the present case, R6G as a model dye was used and its evolution of UV–Visible spectra is illustrated in Fig. 8 at the wavelength of 525nm (λmax =525nm) during the degradation. And NaBH4 acted as a cocatalyst with the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag in the catalytic experiments. As previously reported [9, 36], R6G aqueous solution showed an absorption band at λmax =525nm in the absence of catalysts. In the present study, UV–Visible absorbance spectra of R6G aqueous solution reduced by NaBH4 solely are shown in Fig. 8a as control. Those samples reduced by NaBH4 combined with 1mg or 2mg of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids are shown in Figs. 8b and c, respectively. It can be seen clearly that the degradation efficiency of R6G solution only attained 30% after 3h without Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids. However, the absorbance of R6G solution quickly decreased after adding 1mg of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids (Fig. 8b), while the degradation efficiency of R6G solution increased up to 89% in 10min after adding 2mg of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids into the solution (Fig. 8c). The results demonstrate that the presence of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids can greatly improve the degradation rate of R6G comparable to that of Ag nanoparticles on polystyrene spheres [36].

It is well known that the adsorption of reactants on the surface of the catalyst affects the photocatalytic efficiency. Therefore, tubular nanomaterials, e.g., TiO2 nanotubes (TNT), not only own high specific surface area but also results in special diffusion paths for reactants entering the tubular depth and efficient interfacial charge-transfer. Therefore, TNT is currently considered one of the most promising photocatalysts for water photocatalytic decontamination via destroying the unwanted and harmful organic compounds in contaminated water [37–44]. However, TNT is photoactive in the UV region (<400nm). In our work, the tube-like Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids showed a better photocatalytic behavior under visible light than those reported TNT. The main reason may be attributed to the advantage that the PANI coating on the outer surface of Fe3O4 NTs can prevent the aggregation of the attached Ag nanoparticles, and depositing Ag nanoparticles to the PANI will form N–Ag coordination bonds, which can effectively reduce the possibility of photo hole–electron pair recombination under visible light irradiation. Besides this advantage, our Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag hybrids are magnetic at room temperature, which means they can be recycled by a simple magnetic separation whereas powdery TNTs are hardly to be removed from the treated water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CLSM images of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: As previously reported [28], the CDs prepared by pyrolysis of konjac flour showed strong, multi-colored and tunable fluorescence as well as good dispersity in water. In order to improve solubility of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids, endow them strong and multi-colored fluorescence, CDs were introduced into the hybrids due to increasing interaction of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms between CDs and PANI [23, 28]. CLSM was used to investigate fluorescence properties of the obtained Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs hybrids at various excitations of 405nm, 448nm, and 550nm [25, 27]. As shown in Fig. 9 , the CLSM results indicate that the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs hybrids also have a strong, multi-colored fluorescent property, which can be applied in optical devices, magnetic separation and biosensors [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS of Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs/Ag hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: Recently the fabrication of Ag nanostructures on the conjugated PANI film for SERS has been demonstrated and the intensive interface between noble metal and polymer is expected to induce a specific response in the SERS spectra [8, 46–51]. As shown in Fig. 10 , Raman spectra of the various hybrids present typical D (at 1376cm−1) and G (at 1608cm−1) bands of graphitic materials. It is well-known that the intensity ratio of the D and G bands (ID/IG) can reflect the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon atoms in the material [24, 52]. There are obvious enhancement in the intensity of the vibration signals for both Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs/Ag hybrids in comparison with Fe3O4 NTs/PANI and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs hybrids. The remarkably enhanced Raman signals of the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs/Ag hybrids indicate that both of them can be used as the sensitive SERS substrates for potential biomedicine and environmental analysis.

In summary, hollow Fe3O4 NTs/PANI, Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs/Ag hybrids were prepared using AAO template, sol–gel method, in-situ polymerization and glucose reduction. The Fe3O4 NTs/PANI binary hybrids exhibited high surface area, good dispersion in water, and high coercivitiy due to the presence of inner hollow Fe3O4 nanotubes. For multifunctional performances, Ag nanoparticles and CDs were introduced into the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI hybrids in sequence and the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag and Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs/Ag hybrids were obtained. In particular, the multifunctional Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs/Ag hybrids showed a good photocatalytic property, multi-colored fluorescence and SERS sensitivity in addition to the magnetic property that can be recycling used through magnetic separation. All these advantages make the Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/CDs/Ag hybrids have a charming future in waste water treatment, photocatalyst, biomedicine and environmental analysis.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
Degradation rates of R6G aqueous solution vs irradiation time in presence of (■) NaBH4, (●) NaBH4 +1mg Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids, (▲) NaBH4 +2mg Fe3O4 NTs/PANI/Ag ternary hybrids, (▼) KBH4 +PS/Ag [extracted from ref. S1], respectively.
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2015.09.029.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financed by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51272002 and No. 51403002), the Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation (1208085ME87, 1508085ME102), and the Technology Foundation for Selected Overseas Chinese Scholar, the Ministry of Personnel of China (No.[2013]-385). All co-authors thank the key laboratory of environment-friendly polymer materials of Anhui province.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetic iron oxide nanoworms for tumor targeting and imaging
Facile fabrication of magnetic chitosan beads of fast kinetics and high capacity for copper removal
Fe3O4 nanostructures: synthesis, growth mechanism, properties and applications
Iron oxide nanotubes for magnetically guided delivery and pH-activated release of insoluble anticancer drugs
